coredata.dc:description: Amikacin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic widely used to treat various bacterial infections in humans. However, elevated concentrations of amikacin can damage the cochlear nerve. Thus, accurate and rapid amikacin detection is crucial. In this study, we developed an “on-off” fluorescence nanosensor for highly sensitive amikacin determination based on a composite of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The method quenches CQD fluorescence (turn-off) when they bind to AuNPs but restores it (turn-on) when amikacin binds and releases the CQDs. Adding Cu2+ enhances sensitivity by cross-linking amikacin-coated AuNPs. Under optimal conditions (pH 4, 1 mM Na2SO4, 1 mM CuSO4), the method achieved a low detection limit of 3.5 × 10-11 M (0.02 ppb), a wide linear range (10-10 to 10-8 M), high precision (RSD < 5 %), and a rapid 2-minute response time. Exceptional selectivity was observed over other antibiotics. The CQDs/AuNPs-based sensor successfully detected amikacin in pharmaceutical and surface water samples. This approach offers a fast on-site analytical method for amikacin detection, with potential applications in clinical and environmental settings.

head.ce:title: A highly sensitive fluorescence nanosensor for determination of amikacin antibiotics using composites of carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amikacin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic widely used to treat various bacterial infections in humans. However, elevated concentrations of amikacin can damage the cochlear nerve. Thus, accurate and rapid amikacin detection is crucial. In this study, we developed an “on-off” fluorescence nanosensor for highly sensitive amikacin determination based on a composite of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The method quenches CQD fluorescence (turn-off) when they bind to AuNPs but restores it (turn-on) when amikacin binds and releases the CQDs. Adding Cu2+ enhances sensitivity by cross-linking amikacin-coated AuNPs. Under optimal conditions (pH 4, 1 mM Na2SO4, 1 mM CuSO4), the method achieved a low detection limit of 3.5 × 10-11 M (0.02 ppb), a wide linear range (10-10 to 10-8 M), high precision (RSD < 5 %), and a rapid 2-minute response time. Exceptional selectivity was observed over other antibiotics. The CQDs/AuNPs-based sensor successfully detected amikacin in pharmaceutical and surface water samples. This approach offers a fast on-site analytical method for amikacin detection, with potential applications in clinical and environmental settings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Amikacin is a highly versatile aminoglycoside antibiotic with broad-spectrum activity against a diverse range of gram-positive and gram-negative microorganisms, which is commonly employed for treating both human and animal infections. Recent research has demonstrated the benefit of aminoglycosides against respiratory infections and enhancing immunity (alongside antivirals) for COVID-19 therapy [1]. Amikacin has fewer vulnerable sites to enzymatic attacks than other aminoglycosides, which can help suppress drug resistance to similar antibiotics like gentamicin, kanamycin, and tobramycin [2]. However, it can lead to dangerous side effects such as ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity if patients misuse amikacin. Consequently, amikacin determination and quantification in biological samples are essential for therapeutic studies or toxic control [3]. Many methods have been investigated for amikacin determination and quantification, including fluorometry, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), chemiluminescence, and enzyme immunoassay [4,5,6,7,8]. The drawbacks of these noted methods include using chemicals and expensive instruments and the complexity of laboratory techniques. In addition, since amikacin has low optical absorption when analyzed by spectroscopy and chromatographic methods, it is often necessary to derive chemical derivatization that cannot be determined directly. Under these circumstances, developing a straightforward, economical, rapid, highly sensitive, and selective method for amikacin determination still attracts intense study.

Over the past few decades, a novel class of carbon nanomaterials comprising a nearly spherical morphology and a size of less than 10 nm exhibit fluorescent properties has gained significant attention. These materials are called fluorescent carbon nanomaterials [9,10,11]. Carbon nanodots, called carbon quantum dots or CQDs, have gained attention due to their environmentally friendly nature, consistent and reliable light emission, practical methods for synthesis, ability to be modified for specific functions, and economical production techniques [10,12]. Based on their inherent advantages, CQDs received significant interest in numerous fields, such as photocatalyst, bio-imaging, and sensing [13,14,15,16]. As innovative, environmentally-friendly materials with a broad excitation spectrum and modifiable emission wavelengths, CQDs have been used to detect diverse compounds, such as toxins, ions, pesticides, and pharmaceutical compounds [17,18,19,20,21]. These studies are based on the fluorescence mechanisms of CQDs, including fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), photo-induced charge transfer (PCT) [22,23], and inner filter effect (IFE) [24,25,26].

Furthermore, CQDs can act as potent electron acceptors and donors, rendering them potential agents for oxidation or reduction reactions [27,28]. The hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups located on the surface of CQDs play a pivotal role in initiating the growth of metallic nanoparticles [29,30], as they serve as reduction sites. CQDs function as reducing agents in this capacity, similar to citrates, ascorbates, and sodium borohydride. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are particularly effective acceptors for FRET owing to their high extinction coefficient and strong electronic absorption bands within the visible region, making them an ideal choice for coupling with CQDs. In recent years, AuNPs have found extensive use in colorimetric nanosensor applications, mainly due to their efficient fabrication, high stability, rapid detection, and exceptionally high molar extinction coefficient, which attribute confers a considerably lower detection limit (LOD) than traditional colorimetric assays. AuNPs also demonstrate a characteristic change in the local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) that is sensitive to alterations in morphology, interparticle spacing, or surrounding medium, giving rise to a visible alteration in color [31,32,33,34,35]. CQDs and AuNPs have been developed as fluorescent and colorimetric sensors, which were used to detect several metal ions, organic compounds, pesticides, and antibiotics [36,37,38,39].

CQDs/AuNPs composites were primarily synthesized through a multi-step process, which included synthesizing CQDs solution and AuNPs solution separately, followed by the physical mixing of these two solutions. The AuNPs solutions, in most cases, are prepared by reducing HAuCl4 to AuNPs by sodium citrate upon boiling [36,39,40], or occasionally by laser ablation [41]. Our research synthesized CQDs/AuNPs composite by a simpler yet efficient route. CQDs solution was mixed with HAuCl4 solution at room temperature. In contrast, CQDs serve as reducing and stabilizing agents to reduce Au(III) to AuNPs, thanks to hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups located on the surface of CQDs [22]. The chemical reaction between HAuCl4 and CQDs in our method ensured a higher level of homogeneity in the composite. It promoted an enhanced linkage between the CQDs and the newly formed AuNPs.

In the present study, for the first time, we propose the determination of amikacin based on the fluorescence of CQDs and the mediated fluorescence turn-off–on optical sensor with AuNPs. This study helps establish a simple and rapid on-site detection method for amikacin antibiotic detection in environmental samples. In this method, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs experiences a considerable decrease (known as the turn-off stage) due to the binding of AuNPs to their surface. In the absence of amikacin, the CQDs/AuNPs sensor is wine red visually due to the plasmon resonance of the AuNPs. However, after adding amikacin regenerated CQDs fluorescence intensity (turn-on stage), the sensor's color changed to black blue rapidly. At the same time, under the UV lamp, its color turned from slate blue to pale blue immediately.

Moreover, the sensitivity of this method has been improved further in the presence of Cu2+ as a cross-linking agent for pairs of amikacin-coated AuNPs, leading to their aggregation. Upon optimization, the optical sensor exhibited high sensitivity and low LOD, facilitating successful amikacin detection in pharmaceutical products. However, when applied to surface water samples, amikacin was not detected until it was spiked into the samples, where the CQDs/AuNPs sensor displayed excellent performance in detecting the spiked amikacin with a high recovery rate (95 – 107 %).

All aqueous solutions were prepared using a Thermo Scientific Barnstead MicroPure system with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ.cm. Tetrachloroauric(III) acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), citric acid (C6H8O7), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), copper (II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) were delivered from Sigma-Aldrich (USA) or Merck (Germany). Antibiotics, including amoxicillin, vancomycin, ceftriaxone, levofloxacin, naproxen, and amikacin, were provided by the National Institute of Drug Quality Control (Vietnam).

A mass of 0.1464 g of amikacin standard was accurately weighed and transferred into a 25.00 mL volumetric flask. The flask was then filled to its calibrated mark with deionized water, resulting in the stock amikacin standard solution with a concentration of 10-2 M. This standard solution was used for subsequent analyses in this study.

This study recorded the UV–Vis spectra using a Shimadzu spectrophotometer at room temperature (UV-1650PC). Fluorescence spectra and intensity measurements were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp and 1-cm quartz cell (F-4700, Hitachi). The crystal phase structure of the samples was analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was used to identify the surface functional groups of the CQDs and CQDs/AuNPs composite. The zeta potentials (ζ) of the AuNPs solution were measured using a Zetasizer Nano ZS to compare the charging behavior under different conditions. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were observed with a JEOL JEM 1010 system. XPS analysis was performed using a Perkin-Elmer PHI-5400 spectrometer.

To synthesize carboxylic acid-capped CQDs, 10 g of citric acid were thermolyzed at 180 °C for 24 h under air. After cooling to room temperature, a high viscosity liquid was obtained and then we dissolved it in 100 mL warm water and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm. The solid obtained was dried and weighed to quantify the CQDs produced. The resulting yellow–brown CQDs solution was kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further characterization and use. The obtained CQDs showed an up to 17.2 % quantum yield. This synthesis method is environmentally sustainable and easily scalable due to using inexpensive and readily available starting materials such as citric acid, a natural and low-cost product produced on a large scale through fermentation, and the low operating temperature.

To synthesize the CQDs/AuNPs composite, 375 μL of 10 mM, HAuCl4 was added to 5 mL of CQDs solution with a concentration of 0.02 mg.mL −1. The CQDs act as reducing and stabilizing agents to reduce Au3+ ions completely to Au in about 150 min. The final solution containing CQDs/AuNPs is wine red, indicating the formation of AuNPs.

Initially, the pH of a 2.0 mL CQDs/AuNPs solution was adjusted to 4.0 by adding 0.1 M NaOH, 30 µL of 0.01 M Na2SO4, and 30 µL of 0.01 M CuSO4. Next, an amikacin solution was added to the mixture, which was diluted to 3 mL using deionized water. After letting it stand at room temperature for 2 min, the color of the solution changed from red wine to black blue. UV–Vis absorption spectra in the 400 to 800 nm range and fluorescence spectra were recorded with excitation and emission wavelengths of λex = 350 nm and λem = 445 nm, respectively.

Pharmaceutical samples: An appropriate amount of the sample was dissolved in deionized water to analyze a pharmaceutical sample. Then, 300 µL of the diluted sample solution was added to 2 mL CQDs/AuNPs solution, followed by 30 µL of 0.01 M Na2SO4 and pH adjustment to 4.0. The resultant solution was further diluted with deionized water to a volume of 3 mL. After letting the solution rest for 2 min, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs was measured at a wavelength of 460 nm.

Water samples: Surface water samples collected from Day river in Hanam province, Lam river in Nghean province, and Ba river in Phuyen province in Vietnam were tested for amikacin. Before analysis, all samples were filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane to remove insoluble impurities. The filtered water samples were then analyzed using fluorescence spectroscopy with CQDs/AuNPs solution to detect the presence of amikacin. If amikacin was not detected in the water samples, standard amikacin solutions were added to the filtered samples at concentrations of 10-9, 2.5 × 10-9, 5.0 × 10-9, and 10-8 M before being analyzed using fluorescence spectroscopy.

The crystal phase structure of CQDs/AuNPs was analyzed using X-ray diffraction. The XRD pattern of the CQDs, as shown in Fig. 1 . a, displays a diffraction peak around 20°, indicative of an amorphous carbon structure within the CQDs. The interlayer spacing in CQDs is 0.38 nm, more significant than that of graphite carbon (0.34 nm), and is attributed to oxygen-containing functional groups in CQDs. These functional groups disrupt the arrangement of carbon atoms and decrease the graphitization level [38,42,43]. Fig. 1. a shows the XRD pattern of the CQDs/AuNPs composite, which reveals four sharp diffraction peaks at 38.5°, 44.9°, 64.3°, and 77.5° corresponding to the (222), (400), (440), and (444) planes of the Au crystalline phase, respectively, in addition to the broad CQDs peak [44]. These results indicate that the mixture of CQDs and AuNPs has been successfully synthesized, and the composite exists in both the amorphous phase of CQDs and AuNPs in the crystalline phase. A remarkable point is the shift of the diffraction peak of CQDs from 20° of single-phase CQDs to 14° of CQDs/AuNPs combination, which reflects the increased interlayer distance of CQDs due to the growth of AuNPs on the surface of CQDs [30].

The FTIR spectra of CQDs in Figure S1.a (Supporting information files) show the peaks at around 3437 cm−1, specific for the O–H group and the C–H bond found on the carboxylic acid. It confirms that CQDs have been successfully synthesized from citric acid. The FTIR spectra of CQDs/AuNPs in Figure S1.b shows a significant change in the absorption peak around 1718 cm−1, corresponding to the C = O bond is no longer clearly defined, indicating that there is a disruption of the carboxylic acid functional group due to CQDs adsorbed on the Au nano surface. In addition, the absorption peak at 1381 cm−1 also notably decreases in intensity in the presence of AuNPs [45]. In the presence of amikacin (Figure S1.c), the intensity in the region from 3444 − 3600 cm−1 increases significantly, describing the presence of the O–H group and N–H bond. Further evidence for a carboxylic acid group is shown by the appearance of two characteristic absorption peaks at 1764 cm−1 and 1710 cm−1. In the presence of amikacin (with an amine group), it will form an amide, and there will be two symmetrical peaks at 1764 cm−1 showing the position next to the carbonyl group (amide 2) while the asymmetric peak at 1710 cm−1 indicates the characteristic of the C = O (amide 1) group [45].

The UV–Vis spectroscopy was also used to determine the characteristic properties of composites. In Fig. 1. b, the UV–Vis spectra of the CQDs and CQDs/AuNPs solutions were measured within the wavelength range of 200–––800 nm. The spectrum shows a characteristic absorption peak of CQDs at 280 nm and a typical plasmon resonance absorption peak of AuNPs at 520 nm, indicating the presence of AuNPs with a size distribution around 30 nm [32,46]. The nature of the absorption of AuNPs in the visible light region is not due to the transition between energy levels but to the phenomenon of surface plasmon resonance. When the frequency of the incident light wave is equal to the oscillation frequency of the conduction electrons on the surface of AuNPs, there will be surface plasmon resonance. When light is incident on AuNPs under the action of an incident light electric field, the electrons on the nano surface are simultaneously excited, leading to a co-phase oscillation causing an electric dipole in AuNPs [47]. The appearance of an absorption band around 520 nm demonstrated the presence of AuNPs on the surface of CQDs, as confirmed by XRD measurements.

XPS characterization was conducted to understand better the surface chemical composition of the CQDs and the CQDs/AuNPs composite (Fig. 1. c, d). The results revealed the CQDs synthesized in their initial state possessed specific functional groups, including C = O and C–O–C, with corresponding binding energies of 289 and 285.7 eV, respectively (Figure S2). In the high-resolution XPS analysis of the CQDs/AuNPs composite (Figure S2), two distinct peaks were observed at binding energies of 87.9 and 84.2 eV, which can be attributed to Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2, respectively.

The TEM image of CQDs/AuNPs reveals two distinct particle types (Fig. 1. f). Larger spherical particles with an average size of around 30 nm are surrounded by smaller particles with an average size of approximately 5 nm. The larger particles (∼30 nm) can be assumed to be gold nanoparticles, while smaller particles (∼5nm) could be CQDs particles (Fig. 1. e, f). The XRD, IR, UV–Vis, XPS, and TEM results collectively confirmed the successful synthesis of CQDs/AuNPs composite, with CQDs effectively serving as the reducing agent for AuNPs.

The stability of CQDs is of great importance for practical applications [48,49,50,51]. The fluorometric method was performed to study the photostability of the CQDs and CQDs/AuNPs. The photostability involving subjecting the samples to continuous UV light irradiation at 365 nm for 100 min was examined (Figure S3). Remarkably, the results indicate no substantial decrease in fluorescence intensity. This finding highlights the robust photostability exhibited by both CQDs and CQDs/AuNPs.

To optimize the conditions for synthesizing the CQDs/AuNPs composite, the concentration of HAuCl4 and the reduction time were investigated. Figure S4 shows the changing of fluorescent intensity with the difference in HAuCl4 volume from 50 to 150
μ
L. Compared with the bare CQDs solution, its fluorescence intensity noticeably decreased to the lowest at 75
μ
L of HAuCl4, indicating the fluorescence resonance energy transfer reached the highest at this concentration. Therefore, the 75 μL HAuCl4/1 mL CQDs concentration was chosen as an optimum condition. In addition, the reduction time is also an essential factor for the efficient reduction of HAuCl4 to AuNPs. Based on Figure S5, it can be observed that the maximum absorbance at 520 nm of the CQDs solution with the addition of HAuCl4 increased rapidly in the first 150 min of the reduction process. However, after 150 min, the absorbance appeared to plateau, suggesting that the reduction from aurate to metal (0) nanoparticles was complete within 150 min. This result indicates that a reduction time of 150 min is sufficient for synthesizing CQDs/AuNPs.

The CQDs/AuNPs sensor comprises two components: CQDs and AuNPs are fluorescent centers and a quencher, respectively. The electrostatic interaction induces the CQDs adsorption on the AuNPs surface; the fluorescence of CQDs decreased (turn-off stage) due to the FRET, which occurs between the energy donor (CQDs) and energy acceptor (AuNPs). The green fluorescent spectrum of CQDs covers the red absorption spectrum of AuNPs. When the amikacin antibiotic is added into this system, it interacts with AuNPs through strong affinity between the –NH group in amikacin molecule and Au surface, which causes the release of CQDs from the CQDs/AuNPs composite. This interaction hinders the FRET phenomenon between CQDs and AuNPs, restoring the fluorescence emission of CQDs and switching to the “on” state, as shown in Fig. 2 . The pale yellow color of CQDs solution turned green under UV lamp, indicating the high fluorescence of CQDs material. However, it changed to wine red with the AuNPs addition, while under the UV lamp, it showed a slate blue color. In the presence of amikacin antibiotic, this composite immediately turned black-blue visually and also pale blue under the UV light (Figure S6).

Moreover, introducing Cu2+ into the CQDs/AuNPs solution enhances the sensitivity and selectivity of the detection of amikacin due to the strong binding affinity between the amine group (–NH2) of amikacin and Cu2+ ions, forming a stable complex [52,53]. This complex strengthens the interaction between amikacin and the AuNPs, resulting in the cross-linking of pairs of amikacin-coated AuNPs and inducing their aggregation, which leads to improved sensitivity. The method to measure amikacin in the CQDs/AuNPs solution involves calculating the recovery ratio of fluorescence intensities (F-F0)/F0, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities at 445 nm in the absence and presence of amikacin respectively, and the concentration of amikacin.

When amikacin was added, the color of the system changed immediately. However, the different factors significantly affected the sensitivity and selectivity of the amikacin detection. To improve the performance of the fluorimetric sensor, we optimized the following parameters based on the recovery of fluorescence intensity in the presence of amikacin antibiotic: pH solution, incubation time, ionic strength, and amikacin concentration.

The “on” stage relates to the interaction between amikacin and AuNPs. Amikacin has four primary amines, substituents on two amino sugar rings. The pKa values of amikacin are in the range of 7.64 to 9.89. At pH < 7, amikacin is in its cationic form, so it can easily interact with negatively charged AuNPs through electrostatic attraction force, releasing free CQDs [54]. According to ζ potential measurement at pH 4.0 (Figure S7), the ζ potential of CQDs/AuNPs decreased significantly from −39.04 mV to −27.18 mV with 10-5 M amikacin added, which proved the adsorption of positively charged amikacin species on the negatively charged CQDs/AuNPs surface. Fig. 3 .b shows that the recovery ratio increases with pH. It reaches the plateau at pH 4.0 and then declines from pH 5.0 to 9.0 due to the protonation – deprotonation of amino and carboxylic groups on the CQDs surface [40,55].

The impact of ionic strength on charged nanoparticles can significantly affect the stability of nanoparticle systems and the interaction between amikacin and AuNPs. This influence is attributed to its effect on the electric double layer surrounding the particles [34,56]. As a result, it is crucial to investigate the charging behavior of the CQDs/AuNPs solution. Typical electrolytes such as NaCl, Na2SO4, KNO3, KCl, and CuSO4 at varying concentrations were investigated to achieve this. All electrolytes enhance the fluorescence recovery by depleting the double charge layer around AuNPs surface and enhancing the electrostatic interaction between amikacin and AuNPs. However, among the investigated electrolytes, Na2SO4 exhibits the highest effect. Fig. 3.c shows the influence of Na2SO4 concentrations from 0.1 to 10 mM on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs/AuNPs solution in the presence of amikacin. Increasing the Na2SO4 concentration gradually increased the fluorescence intensity and reached the maximum value at 1 mM concentration. At concentrations of Na2SO4 higher than 7.5 mM, the fluorescence intensity increases further, but the “on” stage of CQDs/AuNPs is switched on even in the absence of amikacin at these high electrolyte concentrations. This phenomenon can occur due to the charge reversal of nanoparticles at very high electrolyte concentrations, which exceeds the critical coagulation ionic strength. The correlation between the critical coagulation ionic strength and the surface charge density can be elucidated using the Derjaguin-Landau and Verwey-Overbeek (DLVO) theory, which relies on the Debye-Huckel (DH) model. In addition to the ionic strength, the counter ion valence notably reduces zeta potential [56,57]. Na2SO4 has a more substantial influence than Cl- due to the higher valence of SO4 2- than Cl-. This higher valence leads to a larger reduction in the zeta potential magnitude on the charged surface of CQDs-AuNPs, leading to nanoparticle system destabilization [56]. According to the results, 1 mM Na2SO4 was determined as the ideal electrolyte concentration for detecting amikacin using CQDs/AuNPs.

The incubation time is crucial since it allows sufficient time for the combination of amikacin and AuNPs and to release CQDs from the CQDs/AuNPs composite. It was optimized in the 0–20 min range by adding 10-7 M amikacin standard solutions into CQDs/AuNPs solution. As shown in Figure S8, the fluorescence intensity enhanced significantly after adding amikacin within only 2 min. After 2 min, the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly, indicating that the interaction of amikacin with AuNPs led to the release of free CQDs completed within only 2 min. Hence, 2 min was selected as the optimal incubation time.

Interestingly, the inclusion of Cu2+ in the CQDs/AuNPs solution enhances the sensitivity of the amikacin detection method due to the coordination of amikacin with Cu2+ through its hydroxyl and amino groups. This arrangement creates a stronger metal binding site than the aminosugars' aminohydroxyl binding characteristic [52,53]. The presence of Cu2+ serves as a cross-linking agent for pairs of AuNPs coated with amikacin. This mechanism causes the AuNPs to aggregate and consequently improves the sensitivity of the system (Fig. 3.d). The optimal concentration of Cu2+ was determined to be 1 mM, as shown by the significant increase in fluorescence intensity with increasing Cu2+ concentration up to 1 mM. The CQDs/AuNPs solution with 1 mM Cu2+ can detect amikacin even at much lower concentrations (∼10-10 M) (Fig. 4 .a).

Under optimal conditions, Fig. 4.a shows a linear correlation between the recovery of fluorescence intensity and the increasing concentration of amikacin in the CQDs/AuNPs solution. This linear correlation was observed in the range of 10-10 to 10-8 M and was utilized to measure the amikacin concentration in the CQDs/AuNPs solution.

Our proposed fluorescence method for quantifying amikacin using CQDs/AuNPs was evaluated based on several parameters, including the linear range, calibration curve, LOD, limit of quantification (LOQ), and the relative standard deviation (RSD%). To assess the performance of the CQDs/AuNPs sensor, we tested it with various concentrations of amikacin under the optimized conditions mentioned earlier. Fig. 4.a demonstrates an excellent linear correlation between (F-F0)/F0 and the amikacin concentration in the range of 10-10 to 10-8 M, with a linear regression equation of y = 0.302x + 0.175, where × is the amikacin concentration (10-9 M), and y is the fluorescence intensity recovery (F-F0)/F0, with a high correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9992. The RSD% for three concentrations (5 × 10-10, 10-9, and 5 × 10-9 M) were 3.28, 4.55, and 3.68 %, respectively, with good precision (Table S1). The proposed method achieved a limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of 3.5 × 10-11 M (0.02 ppb) and 10-10 M (0.06 ppb), respectively. The calculations were based on multiplying three and ten times the blank's standard deviation by the calibration graph's slope (3SD per slope) to determine the LOD and LOQ, respectively.

Table 1 summarizes the figures of merit for amikacin determination. The results demonstrate that our method exhibits the highest sensitivity compared to other reported methods. Notably, the LOD achieved in this study is significantly lower than that of chromatographic techniques, including HPLC-UV, HPLC with chemiluminescence detection (CL), diode-array detection (DAD), evaporative light scattering (ELS), and so on, which require derivatization of amikacin before analysis. This result suggests that our method may apply to analyzing trace amounts of amikacin in biological and environmental samples. The rapid fluorimetric detection method of amikacin established in this study was also compared with some reported detection methods.

The selectivity of the amikacin determination method was assessed by analyzing a standard solution of amikacin in the presence of commonly used antibiotics (amoxicillin, vancomycin, ceftriaxone, levofloxacin, naproxen) at a concentration of 10-7 M. Fig. 4.b exhibits the effect of these potential interferences on the fluorescence intensity recovery (F-F0)/F0. The results indicate that the fluorescence intensity recovery remained nearly unchanged even at high concentrations (∼10-7 M), with relative errors ranging from 1.08 % to 5.43 % (Table S2). This suggests these species do not significantly interfere with amikacin determination at the studied concentrations. The high selectivity of amikacin over other antibiotics may be attributed to the amine group in amikacin, which facilitates strong adsorption onto the surface of AuNPs through Au-N bonding, unlike other antibiotics, which exhibit a low adsorption preference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in amikacin determination of actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Following the successful synthesis and optimization of the CQDs/AuNPs composite for amikacin detection, our study aimed to investigate the application of this composite in detecting amikacin in two real sample matrices, namely pharmaceutical samples and surface water.

Pharmaceutical samples: The effectiveness of our proposed method for determining amikacin was evaluated by testing samples of 5 different drugs obtained from drug stores in Hanoi, Vietnam. These drugs were in tablet and capsule forms and contained additional ingredients, resulting in complex matrices. Despite the complexity, our method was able to accurately determine the amikacin content in each sample (Table 2 ). It showed good agreement with the manufacturer-provided label contents, with errors ranging from 1.4 % to 8.4 %. This suggests that the drugs were of good quality.

Furthermore, three samples were tested using an alternative LC-MS/MS method, and the results obtained using both methods had a deviation of less than 3 % (Table 2). The statistical analysis has revealed a significant concordance between the two methods in evaluating the results obtained from examining three distinct pharmaceutical products. Notably, the computed p-values, ranging from 0.198 to 0.339, consistently exceed the threshold of 0.05 for a 95 % confidence level (Table S3). These results demonstrate the effectiveness and reliability of our proposed method for determining amikacin content in complex drug samples.

Surface water samples: The ability of the proposed method to detect amikacin in three surface water samples was evaluated. However, amikacin was not detected in any of the samples. To further assess the ability of this sensor to detect amikacin in water sample matrices, amikacin standard solutions with varying concentrations were spiked to the sample. Each spiked sample was analyzed in triplicate, and the average recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs) are presented in Table 3 . The results show that the proposed method has the potential to detect traces of amikacin at the parts per billion (ppb) level in water samples with high accuracy, as the average recoveries of the analyzed samples range from 95 to 107 %, and the RSDs are less than 1.5 %. These surface water samples were also cross-checked using LC-MS/MS system, which offers an advantage in terms of m/z separation but has the drawback of having a low limit of detection (LOD) in the range of a few tens parts per billion (ppb). However, when the amikacin concentration was low (less than 5 × 10-9 M) in spiked surface water samples, the LC-MS/MS method failed to detect it. In contrast, our proposed method yielded reliable results at low concentrations (Table 3).

Statistical analysis was conducted to compare the analysis results of spiked water samples containing a 10-8 M amikacin concentration, utilizing both the CQDs-AuNPs method and LC-MS/MS. The findings indicate the absence of any statistically significant difference between the results obtained through these two methods, as all computed p-values surpass the 0.05 threshold of a 95 % confidence level (Table S4). These compelling results validate the accuracy and efficacy of our proposed CQDs-AuNPs approach in quantifying amikacin content within environmental samples.

Moreover, it should be noted that standard analytical techniques such as the traditional UV–Vis method, fluorescence method, HPLC with UV, or fluorescence detection have difficulty quantifying amikacin since it hardly absorbs light. The derivation is always required before analysis, which is time and money-consuming. Our developed method has a quick response time, is a simple instrument, and allows the quantification of very low concentrations with LOD 3 magnitude lower than that of LC-MS/MS.

In conclusion, we have introduced a simple and fast turn-off–on fluorescent sensor based on the synergy of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). This sensor has shown capabilities for the sensitive and selective detection of amikacin in pharmaceutical and environmental samples. Notably, it offers simple qualitative detection, as evidenced by a discernible color shift from red-wine to deep blue-black in the presence of trace amounts of amikacin. For quantification purposes, we established a linear relationship between the ratio (F - F0)/F0 and amikacin concentration, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of the CQDs/AuNPs sensor at 455 nm in the absence and presence of amikacin, respectively.

Our CQDs/AuNPs sensor for amikacin exhibited an extensive linear range, exceptional accuracy, precision, and a high degree of selectivity against potential interferents like amoxicillin, vancomycin, ceftriaxone, levofloxacin, and naproxen. Our sensor achieved a low LOD of 3.5 × 10-11 M (0.02 ppb), surpassing the capabilities of many current methods. This achievement underscores its exceptional ability to detect amikacin in real samples.

However, our approach still has certain limitations. While excelling in detecting amikacin at trace levels, our method cannot currently detect amikacin and other antibiotics simultaneously. This challenges future research—to design a multi-analyte approach, possibly through the integration of multivariate analysis techniques. Additionally, enhancing selectivity, possibly through incorporating selective components such as aptamers or molecular imprinted polymers, remains an avenue for improvement.

Our findings highlight the potential for cost-effective fluorescence methods employing CQDs/AuNPs composites in determining pharmaceutical active substances with a strong affinity for gold surfaces. Moreover, our developed sensor paves the way for amikacin detection in complex sample matrices like blood, urine, and serum, serving crucial roles in clinical and environmental applications. Continuing research and development in this field promises to expand its applications to other samples.
